item.title: Newly elected Solomon Islands prime minister set to visit Australia in first official trip
item.description: Matthew Wale was elected prime minister in May following a no-confidence vote against his predecessor.

item.title: Reform or rebrand? Advocates say employment system is still unfit for work
item.description: The government has announced what it has described as the biggest reform to employment services in over 30 years. The system has been set up to help unemployed Australians find work. But with the continuation of mutual obligations and private providers, advocates say the changes are nothing more than a rebrand.

item.title: Seven dead, officials on alert as Europe swelters through spring that feels like summer
item.description: Authorities in France say at least seven people have died due to unseasonably warm temperatures, while for the second day in a row, the UK has recorded a new record high for May. Much of Western Europe is still dealing with a pre-summer heatwave, leading to health warnings across the continent.

item.title: Will Australia follow the US and Canada on Ebola travel restrictions?
item.description: The Department of Health says it is "closely monitoring" the situation.

item.title: Federal government sues 3M over PFAS contamination
item.description: The Australian government is suing multinational industrial company 3M in the largest ever claim brought by the Commonwealth. The legal action will seek damages in relation to PFAS contamination across defence sites throughout Australia, alleging the company withheld information about their products.

item.title: Detainees at US immigration centre on hunger strike amid concern over conditions, high suicide rates
item.description: Hundreds of people in an immigration detention centre in the US state of New Jersey are on a hunger strike, according to their families. They're protesting poor conditions inside the Delaney Hall facility. It comes as an investigation found 10 people have died by suicide in immigration detention since President Donald Trump took office at the beginning of last year and launched an aggressive deportation strategy.

item.title: Iran-US peace talks stall, Türkiye targets dissent & Trump’s excellent excuse
item.description: Conflicting reports on peace negotiations between the US and Iran as military tensions escalate across the region. Former political prisoner Dr Kylie Moore-Gilbert joins us to explore what it means for the Iranian regime and its citizens. Plus, Netanyahu vows to crush Hezbollah with an expanding offensive as his own leadership comes under pressure ahead of elections. While in Türkiye, Erdogan moves against the opposition in a fresh crackdown on dissent, and Trump’s patriotic excuse for dodging a family function.

item.title: Fire in Kenya girls' school dormitory kills 16, injures 73
item.description: Local authorities said the fire broke out in a dormitory where 220 girls had been sleeping.

item.title: Is 'baby brain' real? Nearly 300 parents put the theory to the test
item.description: Many new parents describe the mental fog of early parenthood as 'baby brain'. But Australian researchers found a surprise.

item.title: 'For us, by us': Indigenous plea for truth-telling and connection during Reconciliation Week
item.description: Community led events are being held across the country to mark National Reconciliation Week, which runs from May 27 to Jun 03. This year's theme is 'All In' - a call for all Australians to commit wholeheartedly to reconciliation, and to advancing Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander people's rights.

item.title: Benjamin Netanyahu says Israel will expand control of Gaza to 70 per cent
item.description: The Israeli prime minister said increasing control of Palestinian territory will "press" Hamas from "all sides".

item.title: Has climbing Mount Everest become too commercialised and dangerous?
item.description: Nepal's mountaineering community is celebrating the 73rd anniversary of the conquest of Mount Everest amid growing concerns about overcrowding, rising temperatures. Nepal has issued a record 494 permits for foreign climbers, with the number of people who reached the summit believed to be more than 900. Questions are growing over whether the world's highest mountain is becoming too dangerous, and too commercialised.

item.title: 'A battle of resilience': Why these teachers and youngsters are looking at karate differently
item.description: In a Sydney dojo, karate is being used to build more than physical strength.

item.title: House prices are falling. So why are many Australians still struggling to buy?
item.description: Borrowing power is shrinking faster than house prices, leaving many buyers no closer to owning a home.

item.title: Australian flotilla participants join ICC case, alleging abuse in Israeli custody
item.description: Eleven Australians were detained by Israel while on board a flotilla attempting to deliver aid to Gaza.

item.title: Autism may be as common in women as men. Yet many go undiagnosed
item.description: Autism has long been seen as a condition affecting boys. Researchers say that understanding is finally changing.

item.title: Dating apps are turning to AI. These Australians have a better idea
item.description: Dating apps have been losing users in recent years. What's emerging, experts say, isn't just a trend.

item.title: The ancient Japanese skills helping young people combat modern stress
item.description: Almost half of all teenagers in Australia are facing psychological distress, according to recent studies. Japanese martial arts skills are helping some build resilience.

item.title: Report calls for more federal support for new dads
item.description: A report calling for more federal support for new dads was tabled in federal parliament this week by the Special Envoy for Men's Health Dan Repacholi. The report by the Movember Institute surveyed 1,200 Australian fathers, finding 84 per cent said fatherhood gave their life greater meaning and 77 per cent said it helped them express love and vulnerability more. But three of five new fathers said they were never asked about their mental health, while one in five reported feeling more isolated or lonely since becoming a father.

item.title: SBS Speaks to Dr Marianne Jauncey about NSW's only medically supervised injecting centre
item.description: New South Wales’ only medically supervised injecting centre is calling for urgent legal reform to allow more sites to open across the state. It has been 25 years since the centre opened in Sydney’s Kings Cross, becoming the first supervised injecting facility in the Southern Hemisphere. Staff have since supervised more than 1.3 million injections, managed more than 12-thousand overdoses without a single death, and made more than 25-thousand referrals for treatment and care. For more, SBS spoke with the centre's Medical Director Dr Marianne Jauncey.

item.title: Devastating storm set to move across Australia after leaving thousands in WA without power
item.description: The powerful weather event is being described as a "once in five-year" storm and is bringing winds of up to 125kph.

item.title: When Lisa was diagnosed with dementia in her 40s, her partner Luke had to 'beg' for support
item.description: Younger-onset dementia is rare but can occur when some are still looking after children and accessing government support can be difficult.

item.title: 'Very excited': New discovery in Laos cave rescue as Aussie diver outlines next steps
item.description: South Australian diver Josh Richards says miners freed from a flooded cave in Laos had provided valuable information to rescuers.

item.title: Ceasefire hopes fade as tensions persist
item.description: Despite weeks of diplomacy and repeated claims a broader agreement is close, conflict continues across the Middle East. Israeli forces have expanded operations in Lebanon, civilians have again been killed in Gaza, and uncertainty remains over negotiations between Iran and the United States.

item.title: What to know about Beaufort Castle — the 900-year-old site seized by Israel in Lebanon
item.description: The fortress has been a strategic military asset for centuries.

item.title: Labor's problem, One Nation's gain: What's driving the poll surge
item.description: One Nation is now the nation's most popular party a new poll suggests, which could offer lessons for the government.

item.title: Home prices in reverse while Australians rethink retirement
item.description: The Australian sharemarket finished flat ahead of key economic data this week, including GDP figures and US jobs numbers. Ricardo Gonçalves speaks with Elio D’Amato from EnviroInvest about the day’s market moves, rising oil prices and BHP’s record high. Plus, Kelly Power, CEO of CFS Superannuation, explains why Australians now believe they need more than $1 million to retire comfortably and why many expect to work longer before leaving the workforce. Also, housing expert Tim Lawless warns national property prices could begin falling from next month as higher interest rates, a slowing economy and policy changes start to weigh on the market.

item.title: Israel orders troops further into Lebanon following day of heavy fire by Hezbollah
item.description: Israeli troops have pushed further into Lebanon despite the ceasefire announced six weeks ago.

item.title: Melbourne's love of head-to-toe black clothing actually makes sense. Here's why
item.description: Melbourne has built a reputation for its love of black outfits. It's not a random phenomenon.

item.title: The Australian fans paying up to $25,000 for their FIFA World Cup 2026 'dream'
item.description: Soaring flight, accommodation and ticket prices have not deterred thousands of Australians heading to this year's FIFA World Cup.

item.title: Climate change 'loading the dice' as farmers brace for El Niño announcement
item.description: A formal El Niño declaration could pivot many Australian regions back into prolonged dry spells.

item.title: Wage umpire's 'regret' over minimum pay rise as unions cheer, employers cry foul
item.description: Unions had been calling for a 6 per cent increase, which employers branded a "ludicrous" ask.

item.title: SBS News in Easy English 02 June 2026
Minimum wage workers get a pay rise as Anthropic looks to list on the sharemarket
item.description: Around three million low-paid Australian workers are set to receive a pay rise after the Fair Work Commission lifted the national minimum wage and increased award wages above the current inflation rate. Ricardo Gonçalves speaks with Commonwealth Bank economist Harry Ottley about what the decision means for household budgets, inflation and the broader economy. Plus, the ASX 200 edged lower as retailers and shopping centre operators came under pressure following the wage decision, while technology stocks rallied and BHP hit another record high. Alice Shen, CFA from VanEck joins the podcast to unpack the day’s market moves and the influence of another strong session on Wall Street

item.title: CSIRO confirms significant job cuts due to rising science costs
item.description: Executive staff said they can meet climate change research obligations despite restricting their workforce.

item.title: The blue-tongued lizard at the heart of one school's reconciliation journey
item.description: Behind one school's blue-tongued lizard lies a deeper story about kinship and Country.

item.title: 'Some kind of apocalypse': 40,000 people shelter in Kyiv metro amid major Russian attack
item.description: Russian attacks killed at least 22 people across Ukraine overnight. Volodymyr Zelenskyy has warned more attacks could follow.

item.title: SBS News in Easy English 03 June 2026
Australia's economy stalls despite data centre investment boom
item.description: Australia’s economy expanded by just 0.3% in the March quarter, with growth driven largely by investment in data centres while household spending remained subdued and productivity declined. NAB Senior Markets Economist Taylor Nugent joins SBS On the Money to unpack what the latest GDP figures mean for the economy and the outlook for interest rates. Plus, despite the weak economic data, the ASX 200 climbed 0.7% as investors scaled back expectations of a near-term rate rise. Josh Gilbert from eToro explains what drove the market higher, including record highs for mining giants BHP and Rio Tinto, strong gains for the banks and a pullback in technology stocks.

item.title: The World Cup is finding out what fans are worth in football's new economy
item.description: Some fans have been priced out of the World Cup in North America, which some critics say is part of a wider shift in sporting culture.

item.title: Medicinal cannabis patients given green light to drive in NSW
item.description: The state government is opening up legal access to the roads for patients who have taken cannabis medication.

item.title: 'Hidden costs': AI data centres set to consume more water than every person on Earth by 2030
item.description: A United Nations report has highlighted the vast and growing demand that artificial intelligence is placing on the world.

item.title: Israel and Lebanon agree to implement conditional ceasefire
item.description: It comes days after Israel signalled a major escalation in its ongoing invasion of southern Lebanon.

item.title: 'Not the act of a friendly country': Bipartisan anger erupts over 'rotten' US tariff plan
item.description: The United States has proposed a new set of tariffs on 60 trading partners, including Australia, alleging they have failed to impose and enforce forced labour bans in their supply chains. The Australian government has rejected the accusations and says the proposed 12.5 per cent import tax contravenes the free trade agreement.

item.title: Racial furore erupts after video emerges of UK police handcuffing dying stab victim
item.description: A WARNING THAT THIS STORY MAY BE DISTRESSING FOR SOME LISTENERS Britain's interior minister has accused activists of hijacking a tragedy to stir up violence after the death of a teenager who was handcuffed as he lay dying - while his killer falsely claimed a racist attack. Nowak's death has triggered debates about policing and knife crime and has spurred claims by far-right activists and politicians that there is bias against white people in the justice system.

item.title: SBS News in Easy English 04 June 2026
'Pacific ashtray': Fiji rejects Australian billionaire's plan to ship and burn waste
item.description: A proposal to ship non-recyclable rubbish to Fiji from across the region for burning has been labelled "waste colonialism".

item.title: 'Horrific': Penny Wong grilled on investigation into alleged sexual assault of flotilla activist
item.description: Fronting a Senate estimates hearing, the foreign minister was also asked how many people Australia has helped to leave Gaza.

item.title: Can you negatively gear a granny flat? Labor has given an answer
item.description: Questions raised during Senate estimates about negative gearing and granny flats have finally been answered.

item.title: Trump’s Bibi tirade, Hegseth peddles used subs &… lucky testicles?
item.description: Donald Trump loses his patience with Benjamin Netanyahu over Lebanon, while the Israeli prime minister hints at an even bigger endgame in Gaza. Is a new Middle East deal within reach, or the region’s latest escalation heading to another full conflict? Plus, a surprise AUKUS announcement on the sidelines of the Shangri-La dialogue and how an iconic Italian artwork is hanging in there.

item.title: SpaceX’s mega IPO lands as miners drag the market lower
item.description: Elon Musk has revealed plans for a potentially record-breaking IPO of SpaceX, with Australian retail investors set to get access to the offer. Stephanie Youssef speaks with Datt Capital Chief Investment Officer Emanuel Datt about the opportunity and the risks for investors. Plus, the Australian sharemarket falls 1.1 per cent as mining stocks lead the losses, with Robert Talevski from Activam Group breaking down the day’s market moves and what drove the sell-off.

item.title: Israeli strikes kill nine in Gaza, despite ceasefire
item.description: At least 15 others were wounded in overnight attacks that targeted four apartments.

item.title: Zelenskyy seizes opportunity as Russia concedes weaknesses in air defence
item.description: Zelenskyy's proposal comes after heavy Ukranian drone attacks on St. Petersburg prompted the Kremlin's surprising admission.

item.title: Trump announces $981 million support package for highly polluting energy source
item.description: The White House has hosted politicians and governors from coal-rich states to announce further support for "beautiful, clean coal."

item.title: Hezbollah rejects renewed ceasefire in Lebanon as Trump maintains the deal holds
item.description: Hezbollah has rejected a US-backed renewed ceasefire deal between Israel and Lebanon. The leader of the Iranian-backed group says the terms were negotiated in talks that they were excluded from - and they amount to surrender.

item.title: Hanson steps in after Joyce backflips on forced home sales
item.description: After failing to answer questions, a senator was warned to "get some clarity before this gets worse".

item.title: SBS News in Easy English 05 June 2026
Tumbling miners lead ASX to a down week
item.description: The ASX 200 closed 0.7% lower on Friday, extending its weekly decline to 1.2%, as mining stocks came under pressure from a three-month low in iron ore prices. Fortescue, BHP and Rio Tinto all fell, while the major banks also weakened. Bucking the trend was healthcare giant CSL, which jumped 5.8% for its strongest gain in more than four years. SBS Finance Editor Ricardo Gonçalves speaks with Blueberry Markets analyst Zoran Kresovic about the forces driving the market and what investors are watching next.

item.title: As home sellers slash prices, one of Sydney's richest suburbs records the biggest discount
item.description: Despite price falls in certain suburbs, an economist says one factor of the property market remains unchanged.

item.title: Sell before 2027 or hold forever? Australians weigh impact of CGT changes
item.description: Tax changes unveiled in the budget have rattled many Australians. Here's what to consider if you want to sell an asset.

item.title: The $1 million question facing Australians planning for retirement
item.description: New retirement findings highlight growing financial concerns. Plus: Incoming pay rises, and Australia's property market shift.

item.title: This rapper reached half a billion streams with one song. He's still forging his identity
item.description: Sooraj Cherukat, known as Hanumankind, says he's caught between 'multiple worlds'. But he doesn't want other people defining him.

item.title: Torres Strait’s first land and sea conference weaves culture with science
item.description: Traditional owners in Far North Queensland have come together with scientists for the first time to help tackle threats against human-induced climate change. The inaugural conference with leaders from seventeen different Torres Strait Islands is helping shape Australia's next State of Environment Report due in December this year. Reporter Josh van Staden travelled to Thursday Island with support from the Pulitzer Center.

item.title: Interview: Dr Sam Bennett from Grattan Institute on future of the NDIS
item.description: Australians who use the National Disability Insurance Scheme are facing an anxious wait with political gridlock in Canberra. The Federal Government, Greens and Coalition have been considering post-budget measures on tax and the NDIS and there's just one sitting fortnight remaining before Parliament rises for a five-week Winter break. As negotiations continue, Tee Mitchell spoke with Dr Sam Bennett, the Disability Program Director at the Grattan Institute. Dr Bennett starts by addressing the elephant in the room which is what might happen if the proposed changes don't make it through the parliament.

item.title: Putin says 'no point' meeting with Zelenskyy, vowing to double down on war efforts
item.description: Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelenskyy had called for a sit-down between the two leaders to end the four-year war.

item.title: Whose choice is it? The unseen side of Australia's abortion debate
item.description: Some pregnant people face harassment and bullying from family members pushing for termination.

item.title: Australia's fuel supply secured well into August as bowser pressure eases
item.description: But with the government's fuel tax set to increase again, the relief could be temporary.

item.title: Public health agency concerned latest Ebola outbreak could become largest on record
item.description: The World Health Organisation says the outbreak is 'moving fast'.

item.title: This island's population is the same as Darwin's. It's about to play in the World Cup
item.description: Curaçao has the same population as Darwin — and it's competing in the World Cup. What could the 'Blue Wave' mean for its future?

item.title: Players sent off, matches abandoned: FIFA to crack down on discrimination at the World Cup
item.description: The FIFA World Cup 2026 could be a test for the organisation and its commitment to rid football of racism. In 2024, FIFA launched its 'Global Stand Against Racism' with a series of initiatives, including an anti-racism panel, education campaigns, and introducing tougher rules to combat discrimination. With incidents of racism continuing to blight professional football while FIFA prepares for its biggest tournament ever in Mexico, Canada and the US, here's how the footballing organisation's policies may play out.

item.title: King's Birthday Honours 2026: See the full list of awardees
item.description: From sport and the arts to academia and industry, the King's Birthday Honours celebrate Australia's best. See the full list.

item.title: Hundreds of community heroes celebrated in the 2026 King's Birthday Honours
item.description: More than 900 Australians have been recognised for their exceptional contribution to the country in the 2026 King's Birthday Honours List. Here, SBS spotlights some of the many community heroes celebrated with a Medal of the Order of Australia. Among them are a self-defence teacher, a powerful advocate for victims of crime, and an author celebrating historic achievements of Greek women.

item.title: Lindy Chamberlain says jurors 'didn't understand' the evidence in her case. She wants change
item.description: Twelve members of the community can decide the fate of those on trial. Is the jury system still effective?

item.title: Philippines earthquake kills at least 35, dozens missing as thousands wait to return home
item.description: A powerful earthquake shook part of the southern Philippines on Monday, causing at least 35 deaths.

item.title: Philippines earthquake kills at least 35, dozens missing as thousands wait to return home
item.description: A powerful earthquake shook part of the southern Philippines on Monday, causing at least 35 deaths.

item.title: Man arrested in Greece after 27 year manhunt | SBS News in Easy English 8 June 2026
item.description: A daily 5-minute news wrap for English learners and people with disability.

item.title: Violence in Middle East following Israeli strikes on Lebanon
item.description: The Israeli Defence Force says it has struck military targets in western and central Iran, after Iran launched a series of strikes on northern Israel. The latest exchange follows Israeli strikes in Beirut’s southern suburbs, which Lebanese health officials say killed at least two and injured at least 20.

item.title: State funeral for renowned melanoma pathologist Richard Scolyer
item.description: Prime Minister Anthony Albanese has paid tribute to world-renowned melanoma pathologist Richard Scolyer, describing him as a "truly remarkable man" and announcing a state funeral will be held. The 2024 Australian of the Year died on Sunday aged 59, three years after being diagnosed with brain cancer.

item.title: Here's what to watch out for this tax time — and when it’s better to lodge
item.description: This year, the Australian Taxation Office (ATO) has warned against relying on “tax hacks” from “finfluencers”.

item.title: The $400k super line that could define how comfortable retirement is
item.description: If you want a comfortable retirement, it's vital to look at more than one factor from your fund, a new report suggests.

item.title: New to the FIFA World Cup? Here's how football's biggest tournament works
item.description: The World Cup is underway. Here's a simple guide to offside, VAR, cards, Socceroos fixtures and how to watch.

item.title: Pharmacists want prescription powers to take pressure off GPs, but not everyone agrees
item.description: Pharmacists want to reduce need to book a doctor's appointment for a quick script, but GP's aren't convinced.

item.title: Survivors recount experiences after 7.8 earthquake | SBS News in Easy English 9 June 2026
item.description: A daily 5-minute news wrap for English learners and people with disability.

item.title: AI IPO rush meets market jitters as Austrailans rethink where to put their savings
item.description: The global AI investment boom is gathering pace, with OpenAI signalling its intention to eventually join rivals Anthropic and SpaceX on the US sharemarket, potentially fuelling another wave of capital raising worth hundreds of billions of dollars across the industry. SBS Finance Editor Ricardo Gonçalves speaks with Loftus Peak Chief Investment Officer Alex Pollak about what the AI listing race means for investors, while Adam Dawes from Shaw and Partners breaks down a softer day on the ASX after the long weekend. Plus, Matthew Hassan, Senior Economist at Westpac, explains why Australians are turning away from property as the wisest place for savings and what weakening consumer sentiment could mean for the economy.

item.title: The three winter bugs facing Australians — and how to tell which you have
item.description: A 'worrying' trend is emerging in Australia's flu response, experts warn.

item.title: Who will win the FIFA World Cup? SBS experts make their predictions
item.description: Every World Cup sparks debate and bold predictions. We asked the SBS team to pick the champion, underdog and Australia's fate.

item.title: Albanese 'very worried' after renewed US strikes on Iran, as Iran hits US bases
item.description: Prime Minister Anthony Albanese says uncertainty caused by US strikes on Iran is creating great difficulties.

item.title: Albanese 'very worried' after renewed US strikes on Iran, as Iran hits US bases
item.description: Prime Minister Anthony Albanese says uncertainty caused by US strikes on Iran is creating great difficulties.

item.title: Australia's first full stamp duty exemption for some home buyers is coming
item.description: Stamp duty is being scrapped for some ACT buyers. Here's how the rest of Australia stacks up.

item.title: Aussies could be paying 40c a litre more for petrol from July as fuel tax cut set to end
item.description: The government's $2.5 billion fuel excise cut is planned to end at the end of June.

item.title: Six months on from social media ban | SBS News in Easy English 10 June 2026
Long-wanted fugitive fights extradition from Greece to Australia on murder charge
item.description: Australian fugitive, James Dalamangas is fighting to avoid extradition back to Australia, where he is wanted over the alleged murder of George Giannopoulous. The 55-year-old had been in hiding since 1999, until he was arrested earlier this week in Greece. A legal process is underway to determine is he can return back to Australia.

item.title: Board diversity approaching milestone as super funds recover from war sell-off
item.description: Australia’s largest listed companies are more gender-diverse than ever, with only four all-male boards remaining across the ASX 300, but new research reveals progress has stalled in other areas, including cultural diversity, disability and LGBTIQ+ representation. Stephanie Youssef explores the findings with David Evans, Managing Partner at Watermark Search.

item.title: SBS speaks to the Japanese Ambassador to Australia
item.description: Japan is seeking to strengthen its alliance with Australia. Japan's top diplomat in Canberra has also defended the nation's gas export arrangements with Australia. Ambassador Kazuhiro Suzuki sat down with our Chief Political Correspondent Anna Henderson.

item.title: These betting markets are banned here but they're drawing in young Australians
item.description: An 18-year-old trader is among the Australians using banned prediction markets, where niche information can be worth a fortune.

item.title: Iran vows to shut Strait of Hormuz to all vessels following US strikes
item.description: US Central Command has pushed back, saying commercial vessels are passing through the strait.

item.title: Strait of Hormuz closed again as US, Iran launch new wave of strikes
item.description: US forces have launched a new wave of strikes at Iran, following days of escalating violence. In response, Iran has targeted the US fifth fleet in Bahrain and says it has closed the Strait of Hormuz to all vessels, warning any ship that attempts to pass through will be targeted. As tensions continue to build, Foreign Minister Penny Wong has joined international allies in calling for de-escalation during a visit to the UK.

item.title: US plans strikes on Iran | SBS News in Easy English 11 June 2026
item.description: A five minute bulletin for English language learners

item.title: Running dry: Fresh pressure on Ebola frontline as deadly outbreak grows
item.description: Doctors on strike and testing shortages are among the steep challenges for Ebola containment.

item.title: How many times has Trump declared an Iran deal is 'close' — and what does it mean?
item.description: The US president has said a peace deal could be imminent on dozens of occasions. What's behind it?

item.title: FIFA World Cup fever dream, Trump taps his loyalists & India’s cockroach rebellion
item.description: The FIFA World Cup kicks off amid geopolitical foul play. Ukraine says it is regaining territory and pushing for talks with Russia’s Vladimir Putin. Donald Trump continues to reshape US politics, and how India’s satirical Cockroach Party became an unlikely symbol of youth resistance.

item.title: 'FIFA is going to kill football': Some Mexican fans feel locked out by 'crazy' World Cup calls
item.description: Average salaries in Mexico are lower than those of other 2026 FIFA World Cup host nations, yet some match tickets there cost more.

item.title: Can the FIFA World Cup score a win for the global economy?
item.description: With just one sleep to go until the 2026 FIFA World Cup kicks off, SBS On the Money explores the business behind the world’s biggest sporting event. Ricardo Gonçalves speaks with Mark Andersen, Co-Head of Global Asset Allocation at UBS Global Wealth Management CIO, about the economic opportunities and investment themes emerging from football’s global reach. Plus, Tim Harcourt, Chief Economist at University of Technology Sydney, looks at the costs facing fans travelling to the tournament and the broader financial impact. The episode also covers a weaker Australian sharemarket, with Henry Jennings from Marcus Today breaking down the day’s market moves, the impact of rising geopolitical tensions and inflation, and what investors are watching ahead of the anticipated SpaceX listing.

item.title: 'Call you once I reach London': Final promise before Aussie family's search for answers
item.description: Manan's father called to wish him a happy birthday as he boarded a flight. Hours later, the plane went down.

item.title: Not just call centres: The skilled Australian jobs now moving offshore
item.description: The latest offshoring trend is sending different types of jobs overseas, experts say.

item.title: Fireworks, red cards and roaring fans as the FIFA World Cup kicks off in style
item.description: A star-studded opening ceremony marked the start of the FIFA World Cup 2026 in Mexico City as co-hosts Mexico beat South Africa.

item.title: US backs out of promised strikes, claims peace deal with Iran is imminent
item.description: Trump claims "final points" of an initial peace deal have been approved and details of a signing ceremony will be announced soon.

item.title: UK PM under serious pressure as defence ministers resign in anger
item.description: John Healy exited Keir Starmer's government hours before a press conference with Defence Minister Richard Marles.

item.title: 'Feeling the pinch' : charities struggling to meet rising demand for support services as winter hits
item.description: Not-for-profit organisations are struggling to meet rising demand for their services due to increasing overhead costs. The Australian Charities and Not-for-Profit Commission's annual report has found expenses are outpacing revenue growth, as the impact of interest rate hikes, inflation and international conflict make an impact.

item.title: Iran says no final decision on peace deal with US | SBS News Easy English 12 June 2026
item.description: A five minute bulletin for English language learners

item.title: The White House lawn is now a UFC arena. Here's what you need to know
item.description: Paris has the Eiffel Tower. Washington has "the claw" — a nearly 30m-tall UFC structure Trump has joked may never come down.

item.title: Fallout from UK minister's resignation casts doubt on AUKUS deal
item.description: The resignation of Britain's Defence Secretary has thrown high-level talks with Australia into disarray and reignited debate about the future of the AUKUS submarine pact. John Healey quit after accusing Prime Minister Keir Starmer's government of failing to adequately fund defence, forcing the cancellation of a planned appearance with Australian Defence Minister Richard Marles. The political fallout is now being felt on both sides of the world, with critics questioning the stability of one of Australia's most ambitious defence projects.

item.title: World Cup opener: Electric scenes and three red cards
item.description: This FIFA World Cup 2026™ is set to be a major one for key and emerging players. Join your host Haylena Krishnamoorthy for this episode of World Cup Daily | The 90+ Podcast, as she breaks down the opening match atmosphere, Mexico and South Africa's match with three red cards, and South Korea vs Czech Republic's game. Will we see more players being sent off the pitch? And will South Korea see Son Heung-Min make a difference in matches moving forward?

item.title: TACO again? ASX soars on peace deal hopes | SpaceX’s stock market debut
item.description: The ASX 200 has surged to a five-week high following comments from US President Donald Trump that a peace deal between the US and Iran is imminent. But haven’t we been here before? Also, Elon Musk’s SpaceX raises US$75 billion ahead of its much-anticipated Nasdaq debut. For more, Stephanie Youssef spoke with Matt Wacher, chief investment officer at Jana Investment Advisers.

item.title: David Hockney, star of 20th-century art, dies at age 88
item.description: David Hockney, one of the most celebrated and influential British artists of modern times, has died aged 88.

item.title: 'It's happened, it's done': A shark attack, 24 surgeries and an unexpected path to healing
item.description: After 24 surgeries, a young boy is finding his way back through dance and culture.

item.title: Raised on Turkish football, Socceroo Aziz Behich now faces his 'second home'
item.description: Aziz Behich will face off against Türkiye in the Socceroos' FIFA World Cup opener. His connection to the country runs deep.

item.title: Watch: USA v Paraguay ends 4-1 as Socceroos rivals USA run rampant in LA
item.description: A fast and dangerous American team left the Paraguayan side looking shell shocked.

item.title: It's six months since the young people's social media ban was applied. So how's it going?
item.description: It’s been six months since the ban on social media took effect in Australia for children under the age of 16. Since then, a growing number of countries have been taking steps to follow suit. Experts point to the ban's positive effects, but what do the teenagers think?

item.title: Where's the wisest place for your savings? Aussies are having a rethink
item.description: We're turning our backs on a once-favoured place for savings, a new survey suggests. Plus: Interest rates, and a $2.5trn space bet.

item.title: Musk becomes first trillionaire in history as SpaceX shares soar
item.description: SpaceX's debut on the Nasdaq exchange has also made it the biggest IPO in history.

item.title: 'Give us a chance': Why experience isn't enough for many migrants in Australia
item.description: Australia has resettled one million refugees since World War Two. But many remain unemployed or underemployed despite their skills.

item.title: Woman fighting for her life after shark attack at popular Sydney beach
item.description: The woman was airlifted to the hospital to undergo immediate treatment for severe injuries to her limbs.

item.title: Australia is facing an El Niño. Experts say it won't be a 'super' event
item.description: Between 2020 and 2022, Australia experienced a triple La Niña. This year's El Niño won't swing the weather the other way.

item.title: Why the Socceroos may have seen a dingo at their Californian World Cup training ground
item.description: Australians are showing up in unlikely places to help with the Socceroos' preparation for the FIFA World Cup 2026.

item.title: INTERVIEW: Eddie Izzard's unique take on Hamlet
item.description: Eddie Izzard is a multi-talented artist whose career spans film, theatre and activism. Now she brings her unique brand of charisma and creativity to the Sydney Opera House with one of the world’s most enduring tragedies. This is Shakespeare's Hamlet as you’ve never seen it before. Izzard performs on the blank canvas of a bare stage, letting Shakespeare’s words and pure storytelling take the spotlight. Izzard shifts between the play's twenty-three characters with clarity and emotional depth. He's been talking to SBS's Wil Brincat.]]

item.title: Donald Trump says Iran deal to be signed within hours | Morning News Bulletin 14 June 2026
item.description: President Trump says initial agreement to end Iran war expected on Sunday; Calls for more Australians from diverse communities to donate blood; And in sport, the big day arrives for the Socceroos as they take on Türkiye at the World Cup.

item.title: Priya and Rohini's quest for fame exposed the film industry's dark side
item.description: 'Mollywood' movies are loved for their bold storytelling. But behind the scenes lies a darker story of widespread exploitation.

item.title: Former refugees, now at the World Cup: The close mates leading the Socceroos' attack
item.description: Former refugees Nestory Irankunda and Mohamed Touré want to make their mark for the Socceroos at the World Cup.

item.title: The long wait for permanency in Australia's shrinking humanitarian program
item.description: For refugees who reach Australia, safety is one thing. Permanency is another — and it's becoming harder to win.

item.title: 'The need is constant': Red Cross calls for more blood donors
item.description: For families relying on transfusions, donated blood can mean the difference between life and death. For others, the decision to donate is shaped by culture, experience and how well they understand the system. Lifeblood is urging more Australians to donate blood, warning the need is constant and cannot be met without regular donors. But as Australia's population becomes more diverse, experts say the country's blood supply also needs to reflect that change, with some patients needing blood that is more closely matched to their background.

item.title: How the 'beautiful game' brings about beautiful change
item.description: The FIFA World Cup 2026 is now underway, and football is once again at the centre of attention around the world. But away from the big lights and global headlines, there are people using football in quieter, powerful ways to build community and drive social cohesion.

item.title: As voter disillusionment grows, why aren't voters flocking to the Greens in Australia?
item.description: Growing voter dissatisfaction with mainstream politics has led to a rise in popularity for One Nation. The same cannot be said for the Greens.

item.title: Young Australian girl shot dead by police in Pakistan
item.description: An Australian family is receiving consular assistance after a girl was killed and two others injured in a police shooting mix-up.

item.title: Nine-year-old Australian girl shot dead by Pakistani police | Midday News Bulletin 14 June 2026
item.description: Nine-year-old Australian girl killed in Pakistan after police open fire on family rental car; Fears of accelerated Ebola spread after deaths in eastern Congo displacement camp; And in sport, the big day arrives for the Socceroos as they take on Türkiye at the World Cup.

item.title: Calls for culls after 'unique and different' shark attack off Sydney beach
item.description: Calls to cull sharks are growing louder after a woman was critically injured by a shark at one of Australia's best-known beaches.

item.title: Australia 'relies on immigration' and restricting numbers could backfire, government warns
item.description: Dramatic decreases in migrant numbers will make the situation worse, the federal government says.

item.title: 'Taken a toll': Rising Liberal star to quit politics
item.description: The Liberal senator has spent 25 years in politics, and said resigning was something he'd been weighing up "for a while".

item.title: Socceroos beat Turkiye 2-0 in World Cup win|Evening News Bulletin 14 June 2026
item.description: Israel weighs its next move as a proposed US-Iran deal draws concern; Hero paddleboarder praised after Coogee shark attack; Australia makes a dream start to the World Cup campaign.

item.title: Irankunda, Metcalfe lead Australia to historic win against Türkiye
item.description: Socceroos' young guns Nestory Irankunda and Connor Metcalfe captured the hearts of Australians at home and at the FIFA World Cup 2026™ with their 2-nil win against Türkiye. In their first FIFA World Cup 2026™ match, Tony Popovic proved his selections - showing that the next generation is one that could define Australia. Join your host Haylena Krishnamoorthy for this episode of World Cup Daily | The 90+ Podcast, as she breaks down the match. Will Australia be able to win again next weekend against host nation USA? And will Brazil's Vinicius Jr keep their team's unbeaten streak alive?

item.title: These Australians chose to be child-free. They want more spaces that don't allow kids
item.description: With the fertility rate at a record low and more Australians choosing not to have kids, some want more child-free spaces.

item.title: Why the safest path to a high salary may no longer be a degree
item.description: The advice for many has long been "get a degree to earn more" — but the labour market is also rewarding vocational skills.

item.title: Fans rejoice at Socceroos' World Cup win | Morning News Bulletin 15 June 2026
item.description: *** Australia closer to the knock out stage after first win at world cup... *** Fears strikes on Lebanon could compromise a US peace deal with Iran... And in basketball, *** The New York Knicks stun with their first NBA win in half a century.

item.title: 'Let the oil flow!': US and Iran reach long-awaited peace deal
item.description: United States President Donald Trump announced the deal, despite earlier strikes threatening talks.

item.title: Fuel excise will be reinstated after June 30
Victory at last for the New York Knicks
Sweden crush Tunisia, Ecuador lose to Côte D'Ivoire, and small Curaçao's big World Cup debut
item.description: In other World Cup matches, Japan clutched a draw with the Netherlands and Curaçao scored in their first-ever World Cup appearance.

item.title: 'Sadistic': AFP boss warns of growing threat coercing kids to harm themselves — and others
item.description: AFP commissioner Krissy Barrett spoke of the "worrying" new trend as she prepares to meet with global law enforcement agencies.

item.title: 'Use this opportunity': Anthony Albanese reacts as details of US-Iran peace deal emerge
item.description: Here's what we know about the deal so far, as some countries signal they could ease sanctions on the Middle Eastern nation.

item.title: War ends tonight, Iran confirms | Midday News Bulletin 15 June 2026
item.description: Iran confirms war with the US and Israel will end tonight, with months of negotiations ahead; calls for Australia to investigate the death of a child killed in an alleged botched police operation; and the Netherlands and Japan draw 2-all in a nailbiting Texas showdown at the World Cup.

item.title: US-Iran peace deal welcomed by nations and UN official | SBS News Easy English 15 June 2026
item.description: A 5-minute bulletin for English language learners.

item.title: Trump says 'let the oil flow'. But will the US-Iran peace deal bring Australians relief?
item.description: The US-Iran deal has eased fears of an oil shock, but what it means for Australians is complicated.

item.title: Anthony Albanese welcomes US-Iran peace deal | Evening News Bulletin 15 June 2026
item.description: Anthony Albanese welcomes US-Iran deal but warns recovery will take time; a Pakistani police officer who allegedly shot nine-year-old Australian girl arrested; in football, Sweden and the Ivory Coast secure opening World Cup wins.

item.title: Patrick Beach's World Cup call-up surprised nearly everyone — just not those who knew
item.description: The young Socceroos goalkeeper surprised the world with a string of confident saves against Türkiye. So who is Patrick Beach?

item.title: Unlocking housing or freezing supply? Labor's tax reforms face first day of Senate inquiry
item.description: Proposed changes to the CGT discount and negative gearing were both criticised and praised for how they might affect housing.

item.title: Comfortable retirement costs less than many think as shares rally on Middle-East truce
item.description: The Australian sharemarket jumped 1.3 per cent to its highest close since mid-April after the US and Iran agreed to an interim deal to end hostilities in the Middle East, easing investor concerns and sending oil prices sharply lower. In this episode, Daniel Martin from Alvia Asset Partners breaks down the market rally, the impact of falling oil prices, and why airlines, miners and other major stocks benefited from the improving global outlook. Plus ASFA CEO Mary Delahunty explains why many Australians may be overestimating how much they need for a comfortable retirement, with new figures showing singles require around $630,000 in super and couples $730,000 by retirement age.

item.title: Socceroos, lions and revolutionaries: The powerful stories of World Cup team nicknames
item.description: Revolutionaries, kings and Indigenous peoples inspired some of the World Cup's most famous team nicknames.

item.title: New super figure Aussies need to retire comfortably — and whether you're on track
item.description: The figure has jumped by thousands — but many Australians still overestimate how much they'll require by the time they leave work.

item.title: Could Israeli attacks on Lebanon derail peace deal? | Morning News Bulletin 16 June 2026
item.description: ** Concerns over the US-Iran peace deal as Israel refuses to withdraw troops from southern Lebanon... ** Perth school mourns nine-year-old Hania Ahmed after Pakistan shooting... And in the World Cup, ** A shock result for favourites, Spain, against cup newcomers Cape Verde.

item.title: What next for the fuel excise tax?
Britain announces social media ban for under-16s
Norway crown princess' son sentenced to four years in prison for rape
item.description: Twenty-nine-year-old Marius Borg Høiby was found guilty of 34 of 40 charges.

item.title: 'This is not democracy': Protests, tensions flare as Iran's World Cup starts with draw
item.description: Hundreds of anti-regime protesters gathered outside Los Angeles Stadium ahead of Iran's World Cup opener against New Zealand.

item.title: Australian World Cup referee Shaun Evans cleared over alleged 'white power' gesture
item.description: FIFA cleared Shaun Evans after he denied deliberately making the gesture, calling it an "involuntary twitch".

item.title: G7 leaders to discuss US-Iran peace deal | Midday News Bulletin 16 June 2026
item.description: G7 nations meet over the details of the Iran US agreement, a new report reveals a record number of Australians died from drug-induced causes in 2024 and Belgium has drawn with Egypt in its first outing at the 2026 FIFA World Cup.

item.title: How secure is the peace deal between the US and Iran?
item.description: The United Nations has welcomed the peace deal between the U-S and Iran, acknowledging that the agreement represents a critical step towards the peaceful settlement of the conflict. But Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu has distanced his government from the peace deal, saying he respects the decision but will continue to protect his own country's interests by taking action against Hezbollah in Lebanon. The peace deal depends on a ceasefire on all fronts of the war - which includes Lebanon. So how secure is the deal?

item.title: European leaders to meet over US-Iran deal| SBS News in Easy English 16 June 2026
item.description: A daily five minute news wrap for English learners and people with disability.

item.title: Australia has built a reputation for horror films. Leviticus is the latest proof
item.description: Leviticus turns queer romance into horror — and into a conversation about how these stories end.

item.title: RBA keeps cash rate at 4.35 per cent | Evening News Bulletin 16 June 2026
item.description: The Reserve Bank to hold rates steady; four Australian Gaza flotilla activists meet with Foreign Minister Penny Wong and in the World Cup, Iran and New Zealand hold each other to a 2-all draw.

item.title: Socceroos dominate national headlines, Curaçao scores its first ever World Cup goal
item.description: Australia continues to celebrate Socceroos impactful debut at the FIFA World Cup 2026™. Curaçao scores its first ever World Cup goal in their match with Germany; Japan came from behind twice to draw 2-2 with Netherlands; Ivory Coast had a late win against Ecuador after Amad Diallo scored at 90th minute; Sweden wins 5-1 against Tunisia in their opening match. Join your host Haylena Krishnamoorthy for this episode of World Cup Daily | The 90+ Podcast, as she wraps up day four of the World Cup. Will Australia be able to keep up its momentum against USA this weekend? And will the US-Iran peace deal announced today bring a sense of calm ahead of Iran’s opener against New Zealand tomorrow?

item.title: Iraq coach Graham Arnold has achieved something at the World Cup no other Australian has
item.description: Graham Arnold has already proven himself a legend to 46 million Iraqis. How far can he go?

item.title: Australia's 'deadliest year on record' exposes a worsening hidden crisis
item.description: Australia has marked a tragic milestone, new figures suggest.

item.title: Historic day of draws: Cabo Verde stun Spain with draw, Iran ties with New Zealand in US
item.description: Cape Verde surprises Spain with a draw in their FIFA World Cup 2026™ debut; Belgium and Egypt drew 1-1 with Romelo Lukaku's electric entrance on substitute. Meanwhile, Uruguay scores their late equaliser, securing a 1-1 draw with Saudi Arabia; and Iran settles for a 2-2 draw against New Zealand in US, amid peace deal. Join your host Haylena Krishnamoorthy for this episode of World Cup Daily | The 90+ Podcast, as she wraps up day five of the World Cup. Will Spain remain as one of the biggest favourites to lift the trophy? What can we expect from Cape Verde in the upcoming matches? And will Iran continue this momentum in their next match against Egypt?

item.title: Digitisation and AI being used to chart the world's plant and fungi population
item.description: More than 400 scientists in 40 countries have used digital tools to help identify plants and fungi species, analyse historical specimens and detect changes linked to climate change. And while AI and the mass digitisation are changing how scientists track biodiversity, major gaps in global data mean only a small percentage of plants and fungi have been identified.

item.title: RBA holds rates but keeps hike risk alive as ASX recovers late
item.description: The Reserve Bank has kept interest rates on hold at 4.35 per cent, but warns inflation is likely to remain elevated and has left the door open to further rate rises. SBS Finance Editor Ricardo Gonçalves speaks with Barrenjoey Chief Economist Jo Masters about the RBA’s decision and what it means for households, businesses and the economy. Plus, the Australian sharemarket recovered from early losses to finish flat after the central bank’s announcement. Stuart Roberts from Stocks Down Under breaks down the market reaction, the sectors driving gains and losses, and what he’s telling clients in the current investment environment.

item.title: US military plans major war stockpile on Australian soil
item.description: The massive buildup of weapons is part of an Asia-wide regional defence strategy to counter the rise of China.

item.title: People tell me I’ll regret not having kids. Why I know they're wrong
item.description: Jess chose to be child-free and had her fallopian tubes removed. She says she feels dismissed when people say she'll regret it.

item.title: Many Aussies want to buy at the bottom. But how far can property prices fall?
item.description: Property prices may be softening, but experts warn trying to pick the bottom of the market is rarely simple.

item.title: The US-Iran peace deal may be Donald Trump's gain — and Benjamin Netanyahu's problem
item.description: Trump's calling it a win. Netanyahu's under mounting pressure. Experts say it might only get the US back to square one.

item.title: Trump 'not happy' at Israel actions in Lebanon | Morning News Bulletin 17 June 2026
item.description: ** Israel-Lebanon dispute threatens proposed Iran peace agreement... ** Notorious childcare predator faces further charges in New South Wales.... And in the FIFA World Cup, ** Aussie coach Graham Arnold to send his Iraqi Lions out to face Norway's Vikings.

item.title: FBI stop alleged drone attack on the White House UFC fight. Here's what we know
item.description: FBI director Kash Patel says "allegedly planned attacks were stopped cold" by authorities.

item.title: Trump says Netanyahu needs to be 'more responsible'
Government pleased at cash rate hold
NSW extradition likely for Queensland convicted paedophile
Government invests in social enterprise program for migrants
G7 leaders show support for Ukraine
Victoria to decide on legal rights to work from home
Russian warship fires warning shots at British yacht
Watch: Magic Messi makes World Cup history with stunning hat-trick; Austria defeats Jordan
item.description: Messi created magic, Austria won for the first time this century, and Haaland made a mark.

item.title: Israeli ambassador says AFP flotilla investigation 'wasting time'
item.description: A group of Australian activists have accused the Israeli military of sexual assault and abuse.

item.title: More Australians dying from drug overdoses than road accidents | Midday News Bulletin 17 June 2026
item.description: More Australians dying from drug overdoses than road crashes; DFAT relaxes travel warnings for some Middle-Eastern countries; And at the World Cup, France and Norway open their campaigns with victories.

item.title: Travel restrictions eased for Australians travelling to some parts of Middle East
item.description: While restrictions have been eased in some areas, Foreign Minister Penny Wong has warned against non-essential travel.

item.title: SBS News in Easy English 17 June 2026
item.description: A daily five minute news wrap for English learners and people with disability.

item.title: 'We don't take s--t either': Socceroos ready for physical battle with World Cup hosts
item.description: The Socceroos say they're laser-focused on defeating a strong USA side, and aren't worried about media, or their previous victory.

item.title: Hanson casts One Nation as rising anti-establishment force in protest-hit Press Club speech
item.description: The One Nation leader said she would seek to ban immigrants from countries that fostered "radical Islam".

item.title: Hanson unveils plans at the National Press Club | Evening News Bulletin 17 June 2026
item.description: One Nation leader Pauline Hanson addresses the National Press Club for the first time... Switzerland prepares to host the peace deal signing between US and Iran, and, at the World Cup... a day for the star strikers, led by more brilliance from Lionel Messi...

item.title: Messi, Mbappé, Haaland: World Cup history, France goal record and powerful debut
item.description: Kylian Mbappé edges his way into the history books - scoring twice as France beat Senegal 3-1; Erling Haaland’s off to a flying start, with a double in Norway’s win over Iraq; and Lionel Messi continues to impress, with a hat-trick for Argentina at his FIFA World Cup 2026™ debut. Who's leading the World Cup Golden Boot? And will there be more discussions after today's VAR controversy on a penalty for Mbappé? Join your host Haylena Krishnamoorthy for this episode of World Cup Daily | The 90+ Podcast, as she gives you the biggest talking points from day six of the World Cup.

item.title: 'Be more responsible': Trump makes fresh jab at Netanyahu amid signs of growing rift
item.description: Once Israel's "greatest friend" in the White House, could Donald Trump's latest comments signal trouble for Israel and the US?

item.title: ASX hits a nine week high as oil prices tumble to three month low
item.description: The Australian sharemarket climbed to a nine-week high despite weakness in energy stocks as falling oil prices weighed on the sector, while BHP and Macquarie reached record levels and ARN Media surged after settling a legal dispute. Ricardo Goncalves speaks with Cameron Gleeson, Senior Investment Analyst at Betashares, about the market moves, Wall Street’s latest record close and SpaceX’s continued momentum. Plus, Stephanie Youssef speaks with CommBank Head of Australian Economics Belinda Allen about a rebound in recreation and hospitality spending, what it reveals about consumer confidence, and what it could mean for the next move in interest rates.

item.title: 'The whole community is sweating': Ghanaians say Black Stars opener will be a party
item.description: Ghanaian Australians are preparing to summon some serious vibes to spur on Ghana's Black Stars to "shock the world".
